speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 22: GitHub Flavored Markdown. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, GitHub Flavored Markdown should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Markdown syntax, GitHub extensions, and writing accessible documentation. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: ALEX
text: From First Paragraph to Polished Repository - Everything You Need to Know: Whether you have never written a single line of Markdown or you already know the basics and want to master the GitHub-specific extensions, this guide takes you from zero to confident. The next useful detail is concrete: We start with what Markdown is and why it matters, walk through every foundational element with examples, and then cover the GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM) features you will encounter in real repositories - alert blocks, Mermaid diagrams, math, footnotes, and.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Use heading navigation (H key) to jump between numbered sections -- each topic is an h2 heading. Every section shows raw Markdown first, then rendered output, then screen reader behavior notes. The Table of Contents at the top has anchor links -- activate any link to jump directly to that section. Code blocks show the raw Markdown to type -- increase zoom and the monospace font stays readable. Each section follows the same pattern: explanation, raw code, rendered result, and accessibility notes. Use Ctrl+F to search for a specific Markdown element (e.g., search "table" or "heading").

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat 1. What Is Markdown? as decoration. Markdown is a lightweight way to format plain text so it renders as rich, structured content - headings, bold text, links, lists, code blocks, tables, and more. That is not trivia. You write in a plain text file using simple punctuation characters, and a Markdown processor converts those characters into formatted output. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, A brief history is still useful because john Gruber created Markdown in 2004 with the goal of making a format that is "as easy to read and write as plain text." Since then, Markdown has become the default writing format.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. GitHub (README files, issues, pull requests, comments, wikis, discussions). Stack Overflow and many developer forums. Static site generators (Jekyll, Hugo, Gatsby). Note-taking apps (Obsidian, Notion, Bear). Documentation systems (MkDocs, Docusaurus, Read the Docs). Chat platforms (Slack, Discord, Microsoft Teams).

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. A paragraph is one or more lines of text separated by a blank line (an empty line with nothing on it). That is the difference between guessing and knowing: If you do not leave a blank line between two blocks of text, Markdown treats them as one continuous paragraph.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because sometimes you want to go to a new line without starting a whole new paragraph - for example, in an address or a poem. That gives the learner a foothold: to create a line break (a in HTML), end a line with two or more spaces and then press Enter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Common mistake - no blank line between paragraphs. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common mistake - no blank line between paragraphs: What renders (wrong): Both lines merge into one paragraph. The next useful detail is concrete: Fix: Add a blank line between them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. Headings create the structure of your document. Put another way, they are how screen readers navigate, how tables of contents are built, and how readers scan for the section they need. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Syntax: add one to six characters at the start of a line, followed by a space, then the heading text. That matters in practice: The number of characters determines the heading level.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Rules for accessible headings. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Rules for accessible headings. Screen readers let you navigate by heading level. This is the part to say slowly: In NVDA and JAWS, pressing H moves to the next heading.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: One H1 per document. The heading is your document title. Every document gets exactly one; Never skip levels. Go from to, never from to. Skipping levels breaks the document outline for screen reader users who navigate by heading; Make headings descriptive. A heading of "Section 3" tells a screen reader user nothing. A heading of "Creating Your First Pull Request" tells them exactly what the section covers; and Do not use bold as a heading substitute. Writing My Section on its own line looks like a heading visually, but it is not a heading in the document structure. Screen reader users navigating by heading (H key in Browse Mode) will never find it. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Alternative heading syntax (not recommended). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: markdown also supports "setext" headings where you underline text with = or - characters. The listener should be able to check this: This only works for levels 1 and 2, and it is harder to scan visually in raw Markdown.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat 6. Emphasis - Bold, Italic, and Bold Italic as decoration. Emphasis changes how text is read by screen readers - it is not just visual. That is not trivia. When text is bold or italic, well-configured screen readers can announce emphasis changes, giving the listener additional context about importance. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX

speaker: ALEX

text: This part earns its place because the following table provides guidance on when to use each type of emphasis. speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: Key terms, important warnings, UI element names means "Click the Submit button". Book or document titles, introducing new terms, gentle emphasis means "See the Contributing Guide for details". Bold italic means Extremely rare - critical warnings only means " Do not force push to the main branch ".

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. Strikethrough text shows deleted or outdated content with a line through it. Put another way, wrap text in two tildes on each side.

speaker: JAMIE

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 10. Links: links are how you connect documents, reference issues, point to external resources, and cite sources. That matters in practice: Getting links right is critical for accessibility - links with bad text are one of the most common accessibility failures in documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Anchor this part on Basic link syntax. Wrap the visible link text in square brackets, followed immediately by the URL in parentheses.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Links with title text. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: add a title in quotes after the URL. The listener should be able to check this: The title appears as a tooltip on hover.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Writing accessible link text as decoration. This is one of the most important accessibility skills in Markdown authoring. That is not trivia. Screen reader users often navigate by pulling up a list of all links on a page (NVDA: NVDA+F7 then select Links; JAWS: Insert+F7). The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Reference-style links. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Reference-style links is still useful because for documents with many links, reference-style links keep the text readable by separating the URL from the prose. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: You define the link target once at the bottom of the document.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Relative links within a repository into plain language. When linking to other files in the same repository, use relative paths instead of full URLs. The useful version is: This way, links work on any fork or branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Email links. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The angle brackets tell Markdown to create a mailto: link.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because GitHub automatically converts full URLs in your text into clickable links, but always prefer explicit descriptive links in prose. That gives the learner a foothold: bare URLs should only appear in reference sections or code examples. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Links. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Links has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Pull up a links list (NVDA: NVDA+F7 then Links tab; JAWS: Insert+F7) to audit your document -- every link must make sense without its surrounding sentence. Reference-style links ([text][ref]) produce identical HTML to inline links -- screen readers announce them the same way, so choose whichever keeps your source readable. Relative links to other repo files (e.g., Setup) work on any fork or branch -- prefer them over full URLs for internal documentation. Links render as blue underlined text by default -- if your GitHub theme reduces underline visibility, rely on the color difference or hover state to identify links. Long URLs in link text are hard to read at high zoom; always use descriptive text like Contributing Guide instead of pasting the raw URL. The title attribute (text in quotes after the URL) appears as a tooltip on hover -- useful at high magnification but do not put essential information only there.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Images in Markdown use a syntax similar to links, with an exclamation mark (!) at the beginning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Syntax, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Syntax: the text inside the square brackets is the alt text - the description that screen readers read aloud instead of displaying the image. That matters in practice: This is the single most important accessibility attribute for images.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Writing good alt text. The following table provides guidance on alt text for different types of images. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Image with a link. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: to make an image clickable, wrap the image syntax inside a link.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat When to use details blocks for complex images as decoration. If an image contains complex information (like an architecture diagram or a data visualization), provide a full text description in a collapsible block. That is not trivia. Screen readers announce images as "graphic" followed by the alt text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Images. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Images has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Alt text is read aloud as the only representation of the image -- write it to answer "what information does this image convey?" not just "what does it look like?". For decorative images, use empty alt text ( ) so the screen reader skips them entirely rather than announcing the filename. Complex images (architecture diagrams, charts) need a full text description in a block below the image -- expand it to read the complete information. Images render at their original size by default -- use browser or VS Code zoom to enlarge them, and ensure alt text is present in case the image becomes too pixelated. Screenshots with small text are the hardest images to read at high magnification -- request that contributors use cropped, focused screenshots instead of full-screen captures. The alt text is invisible in the rendered view but visible in the raw Markdown source -- switch to the source view to verify alt text exists on all images.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put 12. Blockquotes into plain language. Blockquotes are used for callouts, important notes, cited text, and instructor guidance throughout this course. The useful version is: Start each line with a character followed by a space. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. It is used to highlight important information or to quote someone else's text.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because add a on the blank lines between paragraphs to keep them inside the blockquote.

speaker: ALEX
text: Blockquotes with other elements: Blockquotes can contain any Markdown element - headings, lists, code, bold, links. The next useful detail is concrete: Screen readers announce the beginning and end of a blockquote.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Throughout this workshop, blockquotes serve specific purposes. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Screen reader notes: Tips specific to assistive technology users. Learning Room connections: How the current topic connects to the practice repo. Important warnings: Critical information that could prevent errors. Instructor guidance: Notes from the facilitator about pacing or approach.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 13. Inline Code and Code Blocks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 13. Inline Code and Code Blocks: code formatting is essential on GitHub. That matters in practice: the learner will use it for command-line instructions, file names, configuration values, keyboard shortcuts, and actual source code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Inline code. Wrap text in single backticks ( `) to format it as code within a sentence. This is the part to say slowly: Run git status to see which files have changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Command names: git clone, npm install. File names: README.md, CONTRIBUTING.md. Keyboard shortcuts: Ctrl+C, Alt+Tab. Variable or function names: userName, getElementById().

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Run git status to see which files have changed.; The configuration file is package.json.; Press Ctrl+Shift+P to open the Command Palette. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Code blocks (fenced). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: for multi-line code, wrap it in triple backticks ( ` ) on their own lines. The listener should be able to check this: All formatting is preserved exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Code blocks with syntax highlighting as decoration. Add a language identifier right after the opening triple backticks to enable syntax highlighting. That is not trivia. def greet(name: str) - str: """Return a greeting for the given name.""" return f"Hello, {name}! The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Showing diffs in code blocks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Showing diffs in code blocks is still useful because the diff language identifier highlights additions and removals. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: + new line that was added unchanged context line.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. old line that was removed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Code blocks inside list items into plain language. Indent the code block to align with the list item text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to show literal backticks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. If your code contains backticks, use more backticks for the fence. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Use four backticks to wrap content that contains triple backticks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because a horizontal rule creates a thematic break between sections - a visual line across the page. That gives the learner a foothold: use three or more dashes, asterisks, or underscores on a line by themselves. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 15. Escaping Special Characters. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 15. Escaping Special Characters: Many characters have special meaning in Markdown. The next useful detail is concrete: If you want to display them as literal characters instead, put a backslash (\) before them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. The following table lists every character that can be escaped in Markdown.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Examples. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Examples: this asterisk is literal: \ not bold\ This hash is literal: \ not a heading Show a pipe character: \.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on 16. Tables. Tables organize data into rows and columns. This is the part to say slowly: They are used extensively on GitHub for comparison charts, reference data, settings documentation, and checklists. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: Basic table syntax has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Name Role Platform; --- --- ---; NVDA Screen reader Windows; JAWS Screen reader Windows; VoiceOver Screen reader macOS / iOS; TalkBack Screen reader Android. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. How table syntax works has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. The first row is always the header row. The second row contains dashes (---) that separate the header from the data. You need at least three dashes per column. Each subsequent row is a data row. Columns are separated by pipe characters ( ). Leading and trailing pipes are optional but recommended for readability.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Column alignment. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Column alignment is still useful because add colons (:) to the separator row to control text alignment.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move.:--- = left aligned (default).:---: = center aligned. ---: = right aligned.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Formatting inside table cells into plain language. You can use inline formatting within table cells. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Accessibility considerations for tables. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Screen readers navigate tables with Ctrl+Alt+Arrow keys (NVDA and JAWS). That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Ctrl+Alt+Right moves to the next column.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Always include a description before the table. Add a one-sentence summary immediately above the table explaining what it contains. Screen reader users hear the table structure (rows and columns) but benefit from knowing what the table is about before entering; Keep tables simple. Tables with more than 5-6 columns become difficult to navigate with a screen reader. Consider splitting wide tables into multiple narrower tables; Use tables for data, not layout. If you are using a table just to arrange content side by side, use a list instead; and Make the header row descriptive. The header cell is announced before each data cell when navigating by column, so "Platform" is better than "Col 3". The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because if your "table" has only two columns where the first is a label and the second is a value, consider using a definition-style list or bold labels instead. That gives the learner a foothold: this is often easier to read with a screen reader than a two-column table.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Tables. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Tables has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. Navigate tables with Ctrl+Alt+Arrow keys (NVDA/JAWS) -- Right moves to the next column, Down to the next row; the column header is announced each time you change columns. Enter table mode automatically when your screen reader encounters a table -- press T in browse mode to jump to the next table on the page. Before the table structure, listen for the description sentence the author should place above it -- this tells you what data the table contains before you enter cell-by-cell navigation. Wide tables (6+ columns) may require horizontal scrolling at high zoom -- if the table overflows, scroll right to see truncated columns or view the raw Markdown source instead. Column alignment (left, center, right) is controlled by colons in the separator row -- at high magnification, right-aligned numbers are easier to compare than left-aligned ones. If a table is hard to read at your zoom level, try narrowing the browser window -- GitHub will sometimes reflow content or add a horizontal scrollbar.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Learning Cards: GitHub Flavored Markdown has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. GFM features (sections 17-27) work only on GitHub -- your screen reader behavior notes are in each section. Alert blocks (section 18) render as blockquotes with a type prefix -- listen for "Note," "Warning," etc. Task list checkboxes (section 20) are announced as "checkbox checked" or "checkbox not checked". Alert blocks use color-coded left borders (blue for Note, yellow for Warning, red for Caution) -- the text label also identifies the type. Mermaid diagrams (section 22) are visual only on GitHub -- the raw code block is always readable. Use GitHub's Preview tab when writing to confirm your GFM renders correctly before submitting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 17. What Is GitHub Flavored Markdown? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 17. What Is GitHub Flavored Markdown?: GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM) is GitHub's own superset of standard Markdown. That matters in practice: It takes everything from the CommonMark specification (the modern standard for Markdown) and adds features specifically designed for software collaboration: tables, task lists, strikethrough, autolinked references, alert blocks, Mermaid diagrams, math.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Where GFM works. The following table shows where GFM is supported.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 18. Alert and Callout Blocks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub introduced alert blocks in 2023. The listener should be able to check this: They render as colored, bordered callout boxes - great for emphasizing warnings, tips, or critical information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to write them. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to write them has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, start a blockquote. Then, the first line must be exactly [!TYPE] where TYPE is one of: NOTE, TIP, IMPORTANT, WARNING, CAUTION. After that, all subsequent lines start with and a space, just like a regular blockquote. Finally, you can include any Markdown inside the alert: lists, code, links, bold. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Choosing the right alert type. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Choosing the right alert type is still useful because the following table describes each alert type and when to use it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Screen Reader Behavior into plain language. Alert blocks render as elements with an SVG icon and a strong heading.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. NVDA/JAWS: announces blockquote role, then reads the type label ("Note", "Warning") then the content. VoiceOver: reads the type as bold text then the content. The colored icon is decorative (aria-hidden); it does not add information beyond the type label.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 19. Collapsible Sections with Details and Summary. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The and HTML elements create collapsible sections. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: They are one of the most useful tools for keeping long documents, issue reports, and PRs manageable - hiding optional content until the reader chooses to expand it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Important formatting rules. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Important formatting rules has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is blank line after. Put a blank line between the closing tag and your Markdown content. Without it, Markdown formatting inside may not render. Step two is blank line before. Same principle at the end. Step three is the text should be descriptive - it is the only thing visible when collapsed. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Zoom out for a second. What kind of journey is this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Real-world uses in this workshop: The following list describes common uses for collapsible sections.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Bug reports in issues: Collapse the full stack trace so the issue body stays readable. Long configuration blocks: Let readers expand the full YAML only if they need it. Exercise solutions: Collapse the answer so learners try the exercise first. Screen reader guidance blocks: Throughout this course, some sections collapse detailed assistive technology instructions so sighted users see a cleaner page while screen reader users can easily expand them. Meeting notes: Collapse detailed minutes while showing the summary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Details sections that start open. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Add the open attribute to have the section expanded by default.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like; Currently known issues; - Checkbox toggle requires Focus Mode in NVDA; - Alert blocks do not render in email notifications. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen Reader Behavior. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Screen Reader Behavior: the element renders as a disclosure widget. That matters in practice: After expanding: The content is immediately readable.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. NVDA: announces "button collapsed" for the - press Enter or Space to expand. JAWS: announces "collapsed" - press Enter on the summary to expand. VoiceOver: announces "collapsed, button" - press VO+Space to activate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Learning Cards: Collapsible Sections has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The element is announced as a button -- press Enter or Space to toggle between collapsed and expanded; listen for "collapsed" or "expanded" state announcements. After expanding, the content is immediately available -- arrow down to read it; no page reload or focus change occurs. Nested blocks (details inside details) work but add complexity -- each level is an independent toggle button; keep nesting to one level deep for predictable navigation. The summary text is the only visible element when collapsed -- it appears as a disclosure triangle ( ) with text; zoom in to ensure the triangle and text are both readable. Expanded content appears inline below the summary -- at high zoom it flows naturally with the rest of the page content. The open attribute forces a section to start expanded -- useful when the content is important enough that most readers should see it by default.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 20. Task List Checkboxes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: task lists in GitHub issues and PRs render as real interactive checkboxes. The listener should be able to check this: Anyone with write access to the repository can check and uncheck them directly in the rendered view without editing the Markdown.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the teaching move inside Syntax?

speaker: ALEX
text: Syntax has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like [x] Color contrast verified (4.5:1 minimum for body text); - [x] Keyboard navigation tested; - [ ] Screen reader tested with NVDA; - [ ] Screen reader tested with JAWS; - [ ] Screen reader tested with VoiceOver; - [ ] Alt text provided for all images; - [ ]. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave How to write them, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Start with a list item marker (-,, or +) and a space. Then: Add [x] for a checked box or [ ] (with a space inside) for an unchecked box. Next: Add a space, then the task description. Last: The description should be meaningful - "Test NVDA" is better than "Item 3". Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. What makes task lists special on GitHub has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Progress tracking: In issues, GitHub shows a progress bar: "2 of 7 tasks completed". Click to check: In the rendered view, users with triage or write permissions can click to toggle checkboxes without editing the comment. Linked issues: You can reference issues in a task: - [ ] Fix 42 - the task shows the issue title and its open/closed state. PR templates: Task lists in PR templates create checklists that authors fill out before requesting review.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Example - pull request checklist. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Example - pull request checklist has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Pre-review checklist; - [x] Changes are limited to the files listed in the issue; - [x] All new images have descriptive alt text; - [ ] Screen reader tested the changes with NVDA; - [ ] No heading levels are skipped; - [ ] All links use descriptive text (not. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because in Browse Mode, checkboxes are read as checkbox, not checked or checkbox, checked.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. NVDA: Press NVDA+Space to enter Focus Mode, then Tab to find the checkbox, then Space to toggle it. JAWS: Tab to the checkbox, then Space to toggle. VoiceOver: VO+Space on the checkbox element.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 21. Syntax Highlighting in Fenced Code Blocks. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 21. Syntax Highlighting in Fenced Code Blocks: We covered basic code blocks in Section 13. The next useful detail is concrete: This section covers the GitHub-specific syntax highlighting that makes code blocks more readable for sighted users.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Add a language identifier right after the opening triple backticks. Put another way, GitHub uses Linguist to apply syntax highlighting with appropriate colors for keywords, strings, comments, and other language constructs. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Complete list of commonly used language identifiers. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Complete list of commonly used language identifiers: the following table lists the language identifiers you are most likely to use. That matters in practice: If no language is specified, the code block renders as plain monospaced text with no highlighting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Screen Reader Note. Syntax highlighting is visual only - screen readers read the code text without announcing color changes. This is the part to say slowly: The code block itself is announced as a code region.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 22. Mermaid Diagrams. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub renders Mermaid.js diagrams inline when you use a mermaid fenced code block. The listener should be able to check this: They are common in READMEs, PRs, and documentation to illustrate architecture, flows, and relationships.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Syntax as decoration. flowchart LR A[Fork repo] -- B[Clone locally] B -- C[Create branch] C -- D[Make changes] D -- E[Push branch] E -- F[Open PR] F -- G{Review passed?} G -- Yes -- H[Merge] G -- No -- D. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Common diagram types. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Common diagram types is still useful because the following table lists the Mermaid diagram types you are most likely to encounter on GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Accessibility limitation - critical into plain language. Mermaid diagrams render as SVG images. The useful version is: GitHub does not auto-generate alt text for them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to make Mermaid diagrams accessible. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Example - accessible Mermaid usage. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: flowchart LR Fork -- Clone -- Branch -- Commit -- Push -- PR -- Review -- Merge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Write a text description before the diagram that conveys all the same information; Optionally, duplicate the information as a numbered list or table; and Consider wrapping the Mermaid source in a block if the text description is sufficient. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because GitHub renders LaTeX math expressions using MathJax. That gives the learner a foothold: this is common in data science, research, and algorithm documentation. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Inline math. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Inline math: Use single $ delimiters for math within a sentence. The next useful detail is concrete: Renders as: The formula is $e = mc^2$ where $c$ is the speed of light.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Use $$ delimiters for display-style (centered, larger) math on their own lines.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Common LaTeX patterns. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Common LaTeX patterns: the following table shows some LaTeX patterns you might encounter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Screen Reader Note. Math expressions rendered by MathJax include aria-label attributes with the spoken form of the equation. This is the part to say slowly: Screen readers should announce the math correctly, but accuracy varies. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. NVDA with Firefox or Chrome: reads MathML fairly well. JAWS: reads the aria-label text. VoiceOver: reads the aria-label text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 24. Footnotes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub supports Markdown footnotes, which render as superscript numbers that link to references at the bottom of the document. The listener should be able to check this: They are useful for citations, additional context, and references that would interrupt the flow of the main text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How they work. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How they work has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, in your text, add [^identifier] where you want the footnote reference to appear. Then, anywhere in the document (typically at the bottom), define the footnote with [^identifier]: Your footnote text. After that, GitHub renders the reference as a superscript number that links to the definition. Finally, the definition includes a back-link that returns to the reference location. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Footnote identifiers. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Footnote identifiers has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You can use numbers: [^1], [^2], [^3]. You can use descriptive names: [^webaim-survey], [^wcag-reference]. Descriptive names make the raw Markdown more readable but render as sequential numbers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Multi-line footnotes into plain language. Indent continuation lines with two spaces. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Screen Reader Behavior, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen Reader Behavior has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The footnote reference is a link ( element) announcing its number. NVDA: "superscript, 1, link". JAWS: "link, 1". VoiceOver: "link, footnote 1". The back-link at the definition reads "return to footnote reference". Activating the footnote link jumps focus to the definition; activating the back-link returns focus to the original location.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because every heading in a GitHub Markdown file automatically gets an anchor ID. That gives the learner a foothold: you can link to any heading from anywhere - within the same document, from another file, or from an issue or PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How GitHub generates anchor IDs. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How GitHub generates anchor IDs: GitHub converts the heading text to an anchor ID. The next useful detail is concrete: The following table shows example headings and their generated anchors.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, converting to lowercase. Then, replacing spaces with hyphens. After that, removing most punctuation (except hyphens). Finally, removing leading and trailing whitespace. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. You can build a manual table of contents using heading anchor links. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Duplicate headings. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Duplicate headings: if a document has two headings with the same text, GitHub appends -1, -2, and so on to the duplicate anchors. That matters in practice: Best practice: Avoid duplicate heading text.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Finding the anchor for any heading. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Finding the anchor for any heading has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Mouse users: On GitHub, hover over any heading - a chain link icon appears to the left. Click it to update the URL with the anchor; Screen reader users: Navigate to the heading, then Tab once. A link button appears. Activate it and the URL in the address bar updates to show the anchor; and Keyboard (GitHub shortcut): There is no direct shortcut, but you can copy the heading text, convert it to the anchor format mentally, and type it in the URL. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 26. Autolinked References - Issues, PRs, Commits, and Users. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: one of GitHub's most powerful features is automatic linking. The listener should be able to check this: When you type certain patterns in any Markdown field on GitHub, they automatically become clickable links.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Issue and pull request references as decoration. The following table shows how to reference issues and pull requests. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on User and team mentions. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, User and team mentions is still useful because the following table shows how to mention users and teams.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Commit references into plain language. The following table shows how to reference specific commits.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Closing keywords in pull requests. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. When you include certain keywords followed by an issue reference in a PR description, merging the PR automatically closes the referenced issue. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: All of these keywords work: close, closes, closed, fix, fixes, fixed, resolve, resolves, resolved.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because GitHub automatically converts full URLs into clickable links. That gives the learner a foothold: accessibility note: While autolinked URLs work, they create poor link text for screen readers (the entire URL is read character by character). That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE

speaker: JAMIE

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. The element renders text in a key-cap style box. Put another way, press NVDA + Space to toggle Focus Mode.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Press Ctrl + C to copy.; Press NVDA + Space to toggle Focus Mode. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Hidden comments. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Hidden comments: hidden comments are useful for notes to yourself or other contributors that should not be visible to readers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Allowed HTML elements on GitHub. GitHub whitelists specific HTML elements for security. This is the part to say slowly: The following table lists the most useful allowed elements. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Disallowed HTML on GitHub. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub strips these elements for security.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. No JavaScript execution. No arbitrary CSS. No embedded content. No form elements (except task list checkboxes). No input fields. Event attributes like onclick, onload.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat 28. Screen Reader Behavior Summary as decoration. This table consolidates how every Markdown element behaves with screen readers. That is not trivia. Use it as a quick reference when choosing how to format your content.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 29. Accessible Markdown Authoring Checklist. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 29. Accessible Markdown Authoring Checklist is still useful because use this checklist every time you write a Markdown file, issue, or PR description. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It covers the accessibility requirements that make your content work for everyone.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Structure has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] One H1 heading per document, used as the title. [ ] Heading levels never skip (no jumping from H2 to H4). [ ] Headings are descriptive (not "Section 1" or "Untitled"). [ ] Long documents have a table of contents with anchor links.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Text and emphasis. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Text and emphasis has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Bold marks genuinely important content, not just visual styling. [ ] Bold text is not used as a substitute for headings. [ ] Strikethrough text has surrounding context explaining the change.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Links has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Every link has descriptive text (never "click here," "here," "read more," or "link"). [ ] No bare URLs in prose (always use descriptive text). [ ] Multiple links on the same page have unique text if they point to different destinations. [ ] Relative links used for files within the same repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Images. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Images has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Every informative image has descriptive alt text. [ ] Alt text is not a filename (screenshot.png) or generic (image, photo). [ ] Complex images (charts, diagrams) have a detailed text description in a block. [ ] Decorative images have empty alt text.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Lists and tables has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Lists use the correct type: ordered for sequential steps, unordered for non-sequential items. [ ] Tables have a text description immediately before them. [ ] Tables have descriptive header cells. [ ] Tables have fewer than 7 columns (split wide tables). [ ] Tables are used for data, not layout.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Code. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Code has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Fenced code blocks have a language identifier. [ ] Inline code used for commands, file names, and values. [ ] Code examples are complete enough to understand without the visual highlighting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. GFM features has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Mermaid diagrams have a text equivalent. [ ] Alert block type matches the urgency of the content. [ ] summary text clearly describes what is inside. [ ] Task list items have descriptive labels. [ ] Complex math expressions have plain English descriptions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on General. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: General has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. [ ] Color is never the only way information is conveyed. [ ] No information is communicated only through visual position or layout. [ ] Content makes sense when read linearly, top to bottom.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat 30. Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them as decoration. This section covers the errors we see most often in workshop participants' Markdown. That is not trivia. Each one includes what goes wrong, why it matters, and how to fix it. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Mistake 1 - Forgetting the blank line between paragraphs. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Mistake 1 - Forgetting the blank line between paragraphs is still useful because what it produces: One merged paragraph. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Fix: Add a blank line between paragraphs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Mistake 2 - Skipping heading levels into plain language. Why it matters: Screen reader users navigating by heading level will miss entire sections. The useful version is: Jumping from H1 to H4 means H2 and H3 navigation finds nothing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Mistake 3 - Generic link text. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Why it matters: A screen reader user scanning the links list hears "here" - with no context about where the link goes. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Fix: For more information, read the GitHub Documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because why it matters: Screen reader users have no idea what the image shows. That gives the learner a foothold: some screen readers announce the filename, which is not helpful. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Mistake 5 - Using bold instead of headings. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Mistake 5 - Using bold instead of headings: Why it matters: Visually, this looks like a heading. The next useful detail is concrete: But screen reader users navigating with H will never find it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Why it matters: A screen reader user enters the table without knowing what data it contains. Put another way, they hear "table, 2 columns, 3 rows" but not what the table is about.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Mistake 7 - Code block without a language identifier. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Mistake 7 - Code block without a language identifier: why it matters: Without the language identifier, sighted users lose the visual benefit of syntax highlighting, and tools that process Markdown lose metadata about the content. That matters in practice: Fix: Always add the language: `python.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Mistake 8 - Inconsistent list markers. Why it matters: While most processors render this as one list, some treat different markers as separate lists, creating unexpected spacing and structure. This is the part to say slowly: Fix: Pick one marker and use it consistently. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Mistake 9 - Mermaid diagram without a text equivalent. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: why it matters: Screen reader users cannot access the visual diagram. The listener should be able to check this: They hear nothing or a generic SVG container message.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Mistake 10 - Alert block with wrong type as decoration. Why it matters: CAUTION (red) implies irreversible harm. That is not trivia. A suggestion to try a different approach is, at most, a TIP (green) or a NOTE (blue).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes this practice feel low-stakes but still real?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 31. Your First Real Markdown Document - Guided Exercise is still useful because in this exercise, you will create a Markdown document that uses most of the elements covered in this guide. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: This is structured as a hands-on exercise that you can do during the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Setup. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Setup has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Open any repository where you have write access; Click New Issue (or create a new.md file in VS Code); and You will write a mini profile document about yourself. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 1 - Add a heading and introduction. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Type the following, replacing the placeholder text with your own information. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Use the Preview tab (or Ctrl+Shift+V in VS Code) to verify the heading renders as a large title.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like About Me; Hi! My name is [your name] and I am participating in the; GIT Going with GitHub workshop. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because verify the numbered list renders correctly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 3 - Add emphasis and inline code. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 3 - Add emphasis and inline code: Check that bold, italic, and inline code all render as expected.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like My Setup; I use NVDA on Windows with Firefox. My terminal is; PowerShell and I am learning git commands. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 5 - Add a table. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 5 - Add a table has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Tools I Use; The following table lists the tools I use daily.; Tool Purpose Platform; --- --- ---; NVDA Screen reader Windows; Firefox Web browser Windows; VS Code Code editor Windows. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 7 - Add a collapsible section. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step 7 - Add a collapsible section has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Additional Notes;; My accessibility testing experience; I have been using a screen reader for three years. I have tested; web applications with NVDA and I am learning how to contribute; accessibility bug reports to open source projects. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on You are done when. You just wrote a Markdown document that uses headings, paragraphs, emphasis, inline code, links, blockquotes, tables, task lists, and collapsible sections. This is the part to say slowly: Every one of these skills transfers directly to issues, pull requests, and documentation in this workshop.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Your document renders with a clear heading hierarchy (H1, H2). Bold and italic text render correctly. The numbered list shows sequential numbers. The link is clickable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Quick-Reference Card. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Quick-Reference Card has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. This card shows raw Markdown syntax in code blocks -- read each block to hear the exact characters to type. Keep this section bookmarked as a daily reference when writing issues, PRs, and comments. Each code block is labeled by format type (headings, lists, links, etc.) in the heading above it. The card uses large monospace code blocks -- increase zoom and each syntax example stays on one or two lines. Print this section or save it as a separate file for quick side-by-side reference while writing. Syntax characters (,, -, []) are visually distinct in the code font.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat 32. Quick-Reference Card as decoration. It shows the raw Markdown for every element covered in this guide. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on HTML in Markdown. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: HTML in Markdown has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Press Ctrl + C to copy.; H 2 O; x 2; Line one Line two. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Mermaid diagram (with accessible text) into plain language. Next: Appendix D: Git Authentication Back: Appendix B: Screen Reader Cheat Sheet Teaching chapter: Chapter 06: Working with Pull Requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 22. Next in the series is episode 23, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
